symbols: Rocky cave — the desert of Chalcis where Jerome retreated for ascetic life and study
Books and manuscripts — Jerome's monumental work of translating the Bible into Latin (the Vulgate)
Barren rocky landscape — the renunciation of worldly comforts for spiritual discipline
Red cardinal's hat or cloth — traditional attribute associating Jerome with Church authority
description: An elderly bearded man in pale blue robes sits within a cavernous rocky grotto, reading or writing at a makeshift stone desk. Books and manuscripts are visible before him. A small red object, likely a cardinal's hat, sits on the ground nearby. The cave walls are rendered in warm amber tones with dramatic crevices and textures. Through openings in the rock, a distant landscape with trees and sky is visible on the right. The atmosphere is one of solitary scholarly devotion.
theological_description: Jerome (c. 347-420) is one of the four great Latin Doctors of the Church, famed above all for his translation of the Bible into Latin — the Vulgate — which served as the standard Scripture of Western Christendom for over a millennium. He spent years in the desert of Chalcis practicing severe asceticism, and later settled near Bethlehem. Though Jerome himself is not a biblical figure, his life was utterly devoted to Scripture. His retreat to the wilderness echoes the biblical pattern of prophetic withdrawal: Moses at Sinai (EXO.3.1), Elijah at Horeb (1KI.19.8), and Christ in the desert (MAT.4.1-2). Jerome's famous letter describes how, even in the desert, he was tormented by memories of Roman pleasures, and only the study of Hebrew and Greek Scripture gave him peace — embodying the psalmist's declaration: 'Your word is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path' (PSA.119.105).

scripture_refs.reason: God's word as lamp and light — Jerome's entire life was devoted to making Scripture accessible
Christ's wilderness retreat, the pattern for Jerome's desert asceticism
All Scripture is God-breathed — the conviction that drove Jerome's translation labors
Elijah's retreat to the cave at Horeb, a prophetic precedent for wilderness withdrawal
